name: LDL Cholesterol (LDL-C)
summary: LDL cholesterol is the cholesterol carried by low-density lipoprotein particles, often called "bad" cholesterol because excess LDL deposits in artery walls. It is usually calculated (Friedewald) from total cholesterol, HDL, and triglycerides, or measured directly.
whatItMeans: A higher LDL value is the lipid measure most strongly and causally linked to atherosclerotic plaque buildup and cardiovascular risk, so lower is generally considered better. Optimal targets depend on a person's overall risk profile. Lower LDL is usually viewed favorably, though very low values are occasionally seen with malnutrition, liver disease, or hyperthyroidism.
raises: Diet high in saturated and trans fats and dietary cholesterol
Familial hypercholesterolemia and other genetic factors
lowers: Statins, ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors, bempedoic acid
Soluble fiber, plant sterols/stanols, and reduced saturated fat intake
Replacing saturated fats with mono- and polyunsaturated fats
confounders: Calculated LDL (Friedewald) is unreliable when triglycerides are ≥ 400 mg/dL or in the non-fasting state
Recent acute illness can transiently lower LDL
Prolonged tourniquet time can modestly raise measured values

ranges.note: Optimal < 100 mg/dL; 100–129 near-optimal; 130–159 borderline high; 160–189 high; ≥ 190 very high (NCEP ATP III). Targets are individualized by overall cardiovascular risk.

references.title: Cholesterol testing and results: MedlinePlus Medical Encyclopedia
Mayo Clinic Q and A: Cholesterol — know your numbers
